Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan everything around when I could get to the library or campus computer labs. I'd usually try to batch my studying into longer sessions when I had access, downloading everything I could to work on later. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd print materials when possible and work through problems physically, writing things out and creating diagrams. With 19 hours a week dedicated to studying and nine online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about what I tackled when.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on when I could actually get online access and when assignments were due. Some weeks I'd be at the library every day cramming before deadlines, other weeks I barely made it to campus at all because of my extracurricular commitments or just feeling completely overwhelmed. I tried to establish regular times, but life kept getting in the way, and honestly my anxiety made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely had to check assignment deadlines or submit something I'd been working on offline. Sometimes I'd log in just to download all the materials I'd need for the next week or two so I could study at home without internet. The stress of not knowing what was due would build up until I couldn't ignore it anymore, and that's when I'd make the trip to get online.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a normal routine exactly, because my access was so limited and unpredictable. But I did develop a habit of making the most of every login session, checking multiple courses at once and downloading everything in sight. The mindfulness practices I learned actually helped me approach these sessions more calmly instead of panicking about what I might have missed.
Q5: The downloadable materials were absolutely essential for me since I could take them home and study properly. I also found the discussion posts valuable because reading other students' perspectives helped concepts click for me, and I could draft my responses offline and just post them when I got access. The practice problems and interactive elements were great because I learn best by doing, not just reading or watching.
Q6: I often couldn't engage with the video content as much as I wanted to because streaming was difficult with limited internet access and time. I'd try to watch what I could in the library, but 35 minutes isn't much when you're taking nine courses. Some of the supplementary readings I skipped entirely when I was too stressed and anxious to focus on anything beyond the essentials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams, or when it gave me something practical to work with offline. If I could download it, print it, or work through it hands-on, it was valuable to me. I also appreciated activities that helped me understand concepts rather than just memorize information, because that's how I actually learn.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my limited resources and high anxiety about keeping up with required work. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I was genuinely curious or struggling with a concept and needed extra help. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that sometimes made them feel more stressful, even if the content was similar.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline with downloaded files and handwritten notes. I'd print out readings at the library and annotate them at home, create flashcards, and work through problems on paper. I also used textbooks from the library and sometimes studied with peers from my extracurricular activities who were in similar courses. The LMS only saw a fraction of the work I actually put in.
Q10: There were so many times I'd be at home going through printed materials for hours, re-reading my notes, and working through practice problems with no internet connection whatsoever. I'd also meet up with classmates to study together in person, explaining concepts to each other and working through difficult problems. My assignment scores were pretty good because of all this invisible work, even though my login frequency was low.
Q11: This was basically my entire strategy for the course. I'd log in, download everything I could possibly need, and then spend days working through it all offline at home. Sometimes I'd access a video once, take detailed notes while watching, and then review those notes multiple times without ever watching the video again. It was the only way I could manage with no home internet.
Q12: My anxiety got really intense around midterms, and there were weeks where I could barely function, let alone engage with coursework. The combination of high stress, limited resources, and trying to balance extracurriculars sometimes felt crushing. I also struggled when I fell behind and couldn't catch up because getting to campus to access materials felt like climbing a mountain when I was already exhausted.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd prioritize downloading materials over actually engaging with them in the moment. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and the constant stress of managing nine courses with limited resources meant I often used the LMS in a purely functional way rather than exploratory. Confusion about course content sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I was.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos and realize I hadn't absorbed anything because my mind was racing with anxiety. I'd have to go back and re-engage with the same content later when I was in a better headspace. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me recognize when this was happening and take a break instead of wasting time staring at a screen.
Q15: I definitely postponed exam preparation because the anxiety around it was so intense. I'd tell myself I'd study tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow never felt better. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because formulating my thoughts felt overwhelming, even though I knew participation mattered. The irony is that avoiding things made my anxiety worse, not better.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of when I logged in and engaged with the LMS. I'd calculate exactly when I needed to access materials to complete assignments on time, working backward from due dates. This deadline-driven approach meant I sometimes missed out on deeper engagement with content because I was always in crisis mode, just trying to submit things on time.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities and gave me confidence that my offline studying was working. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that course for a while. I wish feedback came faster because by the time I got it, I'd already moved on to the next crisis.
Q18: The structure of having so many online components was really challenging for someone without reliable internet access. Courses that posted materials early and had clear deadlines were much easier for me to manage than ones that released content gradually. I appreciated when instructors were flexible about submission methods or allowed offline work, though that was rare.
Q19: Downloadable resources and clear assignment guidelines made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when courses required synchronous participation or had materials that could only be accessed online without downloading. The sheer number of courses I was taking also made it hard to engage deeply with any single one, even when the design was good.
Q20: I think students might be searching for information, trying to understand confusing content, or dealing with anxiety that makes it hard to actually complete work. Sometimes you can spend hours in the LMS trying to figure out what's expected of you without actually producing anything. It could also be that they're preparing offline and just haven't submitted yet, or they're struggling with the material despite their effort.
Q21: This partially reflects my experience because my assignment scores were good despite low LMS activity. I did most of my actual learning offline with downloaded materials and peer study sessions. I think the LMS activity doesn't capture the real work happening away from the screen, especially for students like me who learn better through hands-on, kinesthetic methods.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you submitted things and basic patterns of access, but they completely miss offline studying, peer collaboration, and the quality of engagement during logged time. They can't tell if you were anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused for a short period. They also miss the barriers students face in accessing the platform in the first place.
Q23: Being engaged means actually understanding and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions of clicking and scrolling. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, applying them to problems, and feeling curious about learning more. It's a mental and emotional state that can happen anywhere, not just when I'm logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I studied at home without internet, the peer study sessions that helped me understand difficult concepts, or the emotional journey of managing high anxiety while trying to succeed. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me cope, or how my resilience kept me going even when I was struggling. My low attendance and engagement scores don't reflect how hard I was actually working or how much I cared about learning.
